title: Quiz: Linear System Applications
description: Test your understanding of applying quantum linear algebra to ML, PDEs, and scientific computing

questions.question: What linear system must be solved for ordinary least squares linear regression?
questions.explanation: Linear regression minimizes ||Xβ - y||². Setting the gradient to zero gives the normal equations (X^T X)β = X^T y. This is a square system that can be solved with quantum linear system algorithms.

questions.question: How does discretizing a PDE on an n-point grid create a linear system?

options.text: The PDE is transformed into frequency domain
Derivatives become finite differences, yielding Au = f
Time evolution is encoded as a unitary

questions.explanation: Discretization replaces continuous derivatives with finite differences. For example, u''(x) becomes (u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1})/h². This transforms the PDE into a system Au = f where A encodes the discretized operator.

questions.question: What is the main bottleneck limiting quantum advantage for classical data problems?

options.text: State preparation: loading classical data into quantum states

questions.explanation: State preparation for arbitrary classical data typically requires O(n) operations, which can negate the exponential speedup of HHL. Quantum advantage is most clear when data is already quantum, structured, or QRAM is available.

questions.question: For kernel SVM, what matrix system needs to be solved?

options.text: The kernel matrix K where K_ij = k(x_i, x_j)

questions.explanation: Kernel SVM involves solving systems with the kernel matrix K where each entry K_ij is the kernel function evaluated between data points. This n×n matrix system scales with dataset size.

questions.question: Quantum linear system algorithms always provide speedup over classical methods for any problem.
questions.explanation: False! Quantum advantage depends heavily on: (1) condition number (O(κ²) cost), (2) state preparation overhead, (3) output requirements. For small, well-conditioned systems or when full solution is needed, classical methods may be faster.

questions.question: What property of PDE-derived matrices makes them suitable for quantum algorithms?

options.text: They are typically sparse with O(1) entries per row

questions.explanation: Discretized PDEs produce sparse matrices with O(1) nonzeros per row (e.g., 3 for 1D, 5 for 2D stencils). Sparsity s appears quadratically in HHL complexity O(s²), making sparse systems more favorable for quantum algorithms.

questions.question: For time-dependent PDEs solved with implicit methods, what is the quantum approach?

options.text: Solve each time step as an independent linear system
Use Grover search to find the solution
Apply a single quantum Fourier transform
Time-dependent problems cannot use quantum linear algebra

questions.explanation: Implicit time stepping (like backward Euler) produces a linear system at each time step. Quantum algorithms can solve each system, though the sequential nature limits parallelism. Some research explores parallel quantum time stepping.

questions.question: Which conditions favor quantum advantage for linear system applications? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Large system dimension n with sparse matrix
Output needed as expectation value rather than full vector
Dense matrix with high condition number

questions.explanation: Quantum advantage is favored by: (a) large n with sparsity for O(log n) scaling, (b) moderate κ to limit O(κ²) cost, (c) expectation values avoid O(n) readout. Dense, ill-conditioned matrices (d) make quantum algorithms slower.
